translation_english:
(Voucher) No. VI.
Account of provisions etc. issued from His Majesty's stores to the Hessian Hospital at New York, namely:
Between May 25th and June 24th, 1777: 9,044 lbs fresh meat, 20,400 lbs bread/flour, 1,176 lbs butter, 2,250 lbs rice, 1,946 lbs oatmeal, 494 gallons vinegar, 6 bushels salt.
June 25th to July 24th: 8,530 lbs meat, 15,000 lbs bread, 1,170 lbs butter, 2,790 lbs rice, 1,624 lbs oatmeal, 390 gallons vinegar, 15 bushels salt.
July 25th to August 24th: 5,286 lbs meat, 8,000 lbs bread, 390 lbs butter, 1,100 lbs rice, 1,120 lbs oatmeal, 260 gallons vinegar, 6 bushels salt.
Total: 22,860 lbs meat, 43,400 lbs bread, 2,736 lbs butter, 6,140 lbs rice, 4,690 lbs oatmeal, 1,144 gallons vinegar, 27 bushels salt.

Calculated Rations:
22,860 lbs meat and bread = 22,860 rations
Remainder of bread (20,540 lbs) at 3 lbs per ration = 6,846 2/3 rations
2,736 lbs butter at 3/4 lb per ration = 3,648 rations
6,140 lbs rice and 4,690 lbs oatmeal at 2 lbs each per ration = 5,415 rations
Total Rations: 38,769 2/3

Note: The above provisions amount to 38,769 and 2/3 rations, with which vinegar has been issued totaling 1,615 gallons and 12 gills. The usual deduction for the same, amounting to £403 17s Sterling, has been charged by me to the Hessian Commissary General in two settlements ending June 24th and August 24th, 1777.

New York, December 24th, 1777.
Frederick William Hecht,
Assistant Commissary General.
notes: The document is a logistical account record written in English Round Hand (Copperplate). It features a clear tabular layout for victualing supplies during the American Revolutionary War. The ink is dark and legible, with some minor foxing. No editorial page numbers are present on the physical sheet. Note the use of 'Do' for ditto and archaic abbreviations like 'vizl' and 'pr'.

1. From 1 February to 24 December 1776, as per General Account: £3389 6s 8d
2. From 25 December 1776 to 24 December 1777, as per the same: £3749 13s 4d
3. From 25 December 1777 to 24 June 1778, as per the same: £1327 13s 4d

Examined and balanced by me, reserving the amount of the regimental accounts for medicine purchased by them in the years 1776, 1777, and 1778. New York, 24 January 1779.

J. G. Lorentz
Commissary General to the Hessian Troops

Hochfürstl: Feld-Kriegs-Commissariat;

Bei Durchgehung derer Litteralien des am 12ten Augst: d: J: Verstorbenen
Lazareth Chirurgi Brand haben sich beyde in originali angeheftete
bezahlte Medicin Rechnungen Vorgefunden, deren Betrag 4 Rth
3 mrg ausmacht. Denne dem Verstorbenen dieſe baare aus-
lage Vor abgegebene Medicin an Recrouten nicht bezahlt worden,
welches aus der Von Herrn Major Hatzfeld eingesandten
Rechnung ersichtlich. Als wird Hochfürstl: Feld-Kriegs-
Commissariat ganz gehorsamst erſuchet, dieſe Rechnungen
in Betracht der armen hinterlaſſenen Wittib und theils-
theils unmündigen Kindern hiewiedrum zu bonificiren,
warum unterthänig bittet.
Der Lieutenant: Müller
Nahmens der hinterlaſſenen Wittib
und Kinder
New Yorck den 15th 7br 1779
translation_english:
Humble Memorandum
to
the Princely Field War Commissariat;

Upon reviewing the documents of the late Hospital Surgeon Brand, who died on August 12th of this year, two attached original receipts for medicine paid for by him were found, totaling 4 Reichstaler and 3 Mariengroschen. Since this cash outlay for medicine issued to recruits has not been reimbursed to the deceased, as is evident from the account submitted by Major Hatzfeld, the Princely Field War Commissariat is most respectfully requested to reimburse these accounts for the benefit of the poor surviving widow and her children, some of whom are minors, for which a humble petition is made.
Lieutenant Müller
on behalf of the surviving widow and children
New York, September 15th, 1779
notes: The document is written in a clear Hessian Kurrent hand. '7br' is the standard 18th-century abbreviation for September. The location 'New Yorck' indicates this was written during the Hessian deployment in the American Revolutionary War. No page number stamp is visible on this side of the sheet.

translation_english:
Note
on the monies paid from the War Chest for medicine received
and consumed, as follows:
1. On 30 May 1777, paid to the pharmacist Otto in Lehe
for medications delivered to the Surgeon Grothe for the use
of the recruits who departed in that month, according to
the receipted bill - 3 fl 17 mgr: or --- 3. 63 3/4
2. Colonel von Wilmorth had to pay for 14 fl of white
sugar purchased at sea at 1 1/2 shillings for the use of the
men lying very ill with scurvy from Captain Lawson's ship,
needed for medicinal extracts -- 1. 1. -
3. Lieutenant Colonel Hattfeld spent in Vegesack for medicine
for the use of the recruits and charged it without a receipt --- 1. 30 ---
4. On 20 March 1778, paid to the pharmacist Behncke in Nien-
burg for medications delivered to the recruits of von Schaller's
transport according to the attached note, 26 Mariengroschen or --- 62 1/4 --
5. On 15 March 1778, the District Surgeon Mandt
at Rodenberg was paid for medicine administered to various
sick recruits according to the attached receipted bill,
1 fl 14 mgr; or --- 1. 35. ---
6. For the medicine purchased in Bremen, Nienburg, and Portsmouth
for the recruits of the 4th transport, paid by Colonel von Hanx-
delen according to the attached receipted note --- 2 10 5 3/4
7. To the Jäger Sergeant Breide for medicine purchased for the use
of the captured Jägers in Rutland on 10 May 1779 --- 10 10 3/4
8. On 23 Sept 1779, the Surgeon Warmus of the
Brunswickers received for medicine likewise administered to the
local Jägers according to the attached bill --- 1 1 9 1/2
Total 7. 11 1/4 s. 4. 1 3/4
That forty-seven thalers, ten shillings, 5 1/2 R.M. as well as
five Livres 4 S. 1 3/4 d. have been truly charged to the
Princely Hessian Field War Chest; which
is hereby dutifully attested by me.
New York, 20 March 1780.
9 On 25 Nov 1777, paid to the pharmacist Moost for the medicine
delivered for the use of the Jäger recruits per receipt
2 fl 30 mgl: or --- 2. 45.
Paid to the widow of the pharmacist Henschen in Bremen for medicine
likewise delivered to the recruits according to the note, 45 Grote or --- 56 1/4
10. 52 3/4

translation_english:
Memorandum

The medicinal accounts introduced under No. X from Kassel in German money, namely:
1) the one for over 3054 florins for the 1st Division, as well as
2) the one for over 1674 florins 11 groschen 3 pfennigs for the 2nd Division, have been [processed] by Apothecary Rudolph to the best of his knowledge; whereas
3) for the one introduced in English money [amounting to] 656 £ 3 s 2/4 d, the local Apothecary Attwood has itemized the medicaments that arrived from Kassel in the current year according to their correct weight, now at local prices, and acknowledged receipt thereof with a signature.
notes: The document is written in a clear 18th-century Hessian administrative hand (Kurrent). It concerns the audit of medicinal costs for Hessian troops during the American Revolution era, contrasting German currency (florins, groschen) with English currency (pounds, shillings). The paper shows minor erosion at the right edge.

translation_english:
Balance
What was received in the years 1776, 1777, and 1778 by the local War Chest for the account of the Hospital, and paid out again against it, as follows:
Income:
1. In the year 1776, charged to the highly esteemed regiments and corps for the personnel cared for in the hospitals — 1500.
2. Likewise in the year 1777 — 3014.
3. Further in the year 1778 — 1176.
4. Various officers and other persons belonging to the corps had to pay for the medicines received from the pharmacies, specifically for 1776, 1777, 1778 — Total 114. 16. 9.
5. The highly esteemed regiments were charged for the cost of medicine received from the field pharmacies to satisfy their medical needs, and the same was collected for 1776, 1777, 1778.
6. For the washing wages of shirts washed by the hospital maids, specifically for 1776, 1777, 1778.
7. For the maintenance of our hospitals, paid in January 1778 — 2000.
8. Several officers on the voyage from Europe had consumed 2 [measures] of wheat purchased for the hospital in Bremen, and were charged for it — 20. 6.
9. Likewise for 1 barrel of butter — 4. 11. 2.
10. In the meantime, the funds credited to the English for 580 Hessian woolen blankets were brought to income here — 507. 10.
11. For the 147 lbs of meat received and given out from the hospital in the year 1777, which were distributed as rations, recorded as income — 5. 3.
Total Income: 9709. 24 3/4.
Expenditure:
1. Paid out of the War Chest for the general management of the hospital for 1776, 1777, 1778.
2. Paid for medicines sent abroad in the years 1776, 1777, 1778.
3. Compensated for medical costs, servant wages, and other extraordinaries for 1776, 1777, 1778.
4. The English Commissariat charged for the provisions and rations delivered in the hospital from August 9, 1776, to August 24, 1777 inclusive — reduced to 3623. 18.
Total Expenditure: 9670. 19.
New York, August 24, 1779.
notes: The document is an accounting balance sheet for a military hospital during the American Revolutionary War. It features significant physical damage, including frayed and eroded edges with some loss of text on the right side. A stamped page number '154' appears at the top right. There is evidence of ink bleed-through from the reverse side.

translation_english:
Copy.) No. I.) Account of Expenses for Bed furniture purchased in America for the use of the Hessian Sick and Wounded in the General Hospital at New York between the 1st of January and 31st of December 1777, both days included.

Section I details purchases of fabric (Ozenbrigs/Osnaburg), thread, and the labor for making and repairing straw sacks (bedding). It includes payments to firms like Messrs Hatzky & Co and Ward & Selkrig, and individuals like Corporal Ortstatt and Sergeant Schlechter. Miscellaneous items include bedsteads from Henry Müller and close stools (chamber pots) from John Jones. Total for this section: £155 3s 2 1/8d Sterling.

Section II details kitchen and other utensils. Purchases include tin capsules from Miss Meyer, kettles and tinning services from Charles White, boxes for medicine from Henry Brown, pots from David Mellows, and cups from Daniel Ebbets. It also covers installation costs for kettles (Masonry) and various kitchenware from Mr. Schmidt and George Heydecker. Subtotal carried over: £39 0s 11 1/2d Sterling.
notes: The document is a copy of an 18th-century ledger in English documenting expenses for Hessian troops stationed in America during the Revolutionary War. It covers the 1777 fiscal year for the general hospital in New York. The handwriting is a clear English clerk's hand. Notable items include Ozenbrigs (Osnaburg fabric) used for bedding. Currency is primarily in Sterling, with noted conversions from local New York currency (Curry).

Account of Provisions etc. issued from His Majesty's Stores to the undermentioned Hessian Hospitals, namely:

35,709 lbs of meat and bread = 35,709 rations
Remainder of 40,120 lbs of bread/flour at 3 lbs per ration = 13,373 1/3 rations
4,023 lbs of butter at 3/4 lb per ration = 5,364 rations

Total Rations: 61,224 5/6

Note: The above provisions amount to 61,224 5/6 rations for which no rum was issued. The equivalent value of 2,551 gallons and 1 gill of rum, totaling 637 pounds 15 shillings Sterling (the usual deduction/stoppage), has been charged by me to the Hessian Commissary General across three settlements ending Feb 24, April 24, and June 24, 1777.
New York, December 22, 1777. Frederick William Hecht, Assistant Commissary General.

translation_english:
Balance
What was received and paid into the Princely War Chest for the account of the hospital in the years 1776, 1777, 1778 & 1779, namely: Receipts:
1. In the year 1776, credited to the honorable regiments and corps for the personnel cared for in the hospitals... [amounts in Sterling listed for 1776, 1777, 1778, 1779]
2. Various officers and other persons belonging to the corps had to pay for medicines received from the pharmacies, namely for 1776 through 1779...
3. To the honorable regiments for refilling their medicine chests from the field pharmacies, the received medicine was credited and collected until now for the years 1776 through 1779...
4. Washing wages for shirts and trousers washed by the hospital maid: for 1776 through 1779...
5. In January 1778, paid in cash for the maintenance of our hospitals...
6. Some officers consumed 2 hogsheads of Rhenish French wine purchased for the hospital in Bremen during the voyage from Europe, and were charged for it...
7. Likewise for a barrel of butter...
8. For the time being, the money credited by the English for 580 Hessian woolen blankets was brought here as income...
9. For the meat issued from the hospital stock in 1777, which amounted to 498 portions, then for income...
10. For 2 hogsheads of vinegar sent for the hospital with the transport that left Brooklyn in 1778, which were sold in York [New York], the following was realized...
11. Likewise, receipts were recorded for 4 hogsheads of brandy also found to be sold...
Total of Receipts: 12389 Pounds 18 Shillings 4 Pence.
Against this: Expenses.
notes: The document is a ledger sheet detailing hospital finances during the American Revolutionary War period, specifically involving Hessian forces. Currency is calculated in Reichsthaler (Rthl), Kreuzer (xr), and British Sterling (£, s, d). Mentions 'Hessische wollene Decken' (Hessian wool blankets) and provisions like 'Franzwein' (French wine) and 'Franzbranntwein' (brandy). The location 'York' refers to New York City.

transcription_german:
Perhaps You will wonder at the great Sums of money, laid out
for physic, Drugs &c. but please to consider, that when we
arriv'd here from Europe, we found the best part of the Medicines
brought along with us from Hesse, had been spoil'd at Sea,
and when our Regiments apply'd to H. E. Sir William Howe
for a fresh Supply, Dr. Morris inform'd me, that the small
Stock of Medicines, they had got themselves, would not allow
them to let us have any thereof, wherefore we were at Liberty,
to buy them here as well as they did, tho' these Articles were
then sold here at an extravagant prize —
As I have never yet receiv'd any the least decisive
Answer upon my repeated Applications for the payment
of the Sum due to our Hospital, and thereby have been
disenabled to make my Report of the true State of these
matters to my Superiors at Cassell, I must once more
earnestly intreat You (tho' as to myself I am not in the
least concern'd in it, and neither lose nor win a farthing
by it) but must do every thing out of mere duty to my
Sovereign, to examine all the Hospital's Accounts now
in Your hands, to apply to me, should You find any thing
wrong, or harbour any Doubts about them, and then to
inform me as soon as possible, whether or not the Sum
in Demand shall be paid here or not, or whether the
same must be ordered by the Treasury at London,
In a few Days I hope to send You the Expen:
diture Account for the year 1778. and I am as much
as can be
Sir
Your
most obedient Servant
J. G. Lorentz
translation_english:
Perhaps you will wonder at the large sums of money spent on physics and drugs, etc. but please consider that when we arrived here from Europe, we found that the majority of the medicines brought with us from Hesse had been spoiled at sea. When our regiments applied to His Excellency Sir William Howe for a fresh supply, Dr. Morris informed me that the small stock of medicines they had obtained for themselves would not allow them to let us have any. Therefore, we were at liberty to buy them here just as they did, although these articles were then sold here at an extravagant price.
As I have not yet received any decisive answer regarding my repeated applications for the payment of the sum due to our hospital—and have thereby been unable to make my report on the true state of these matters to my superiors in Kassel—I must once more earnestly entreat you (though personally I am not concerned in it, and neither lose nor win a farthing by it) to perform my duty to my Sovereign by examining all the hospital accounts now in your hands. Please contact me should you find anything wrong or have any doubts about them, and then inform me as soon as possible whether or not the sum demanded shall be paid here, or whether it must be ordered by the Treasury in London.
In a few days, I hope to send you the expenditure account for the year 1778. I am, Sir, your most obedient servant, J. G. Lorentz.
notes: The document is written in English cursive by J.G. Lorentz, the Hessian Commissary of War. It discusses the financial difficulties of the Hessian field hospital during the American Revolutionary War. There is faint mirror ink offset visible across the page. Want of the Originals left behind; For that reason I order'd our Physicians
and Apothecaries, who must know very well, what Medicines, Drugs &c. they
carried along with them in 1776. and what they got from home in 1777. to make
an Account of them, and to put in no other Articles, but such, as the Apotheca-
ries were always ready to make Oath upon, of course to charge rather less,
than more, because the Difference could be easily found out as soon as the
Original Vouchers should be at hand.
Agreeable to these Orders I got the following german Accounts for
Physic, Drugs &c: vizt.
In the General Account of the year 1776. sub Art: X.
a.) To Medicines, Drugs &c: German Money
To Mr. Wild, Apothecary at Cassell No: 32 for . . . . . Rthl. 3054. — 9 gr. — 8 pf
To the same No: 33 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1674. 11. 3
c.) To old Linnen, Bandages &c: for the use
of the Surgeons
To Mr. Bonsfeld, Merchant at Cassell No: 44 . . . . . . 378. 20. 3
To the same No: 45 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 263. 12. —
In the year 1777. sub Art: XI.
No. 92. at Rthl. 4920. 31 alb. or . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 4920. 23. —
Amounting in the whole to Rthl. 10291. 18. 9
At the same time I wrote home to send me the Original Vouchers for those
Medicines, Drugs &c: as well for 1776. as 1777. I received them by the
last Mail: You'll find them here annex'd all together, and the hand writings
very well agreeing with the Original Vouchers of this year's Accounts alrea-
dy in Your hands, which I got a little sooner with the last Hessian
Recruits.
In examining the above mention'd Originals I find, they
differ from my substituted Vouchers in Rthl. 2701. 20 1/4 ggr, or £ 463. 3. 6 d.
Sterling, the whole Sum of the Originals amounting to . . Rthl. 12993. 15 ggr
and there being only charg'd . . . . . . . . . . . . . 10291. 18. 9 d.
I hope, Gentlemen, You'll be pleas'd to add the above £ 463. 3. 6 d.
to the whole Demand of the Hessian Hospital being £ 18737. 16. 1 1/3 d. and
instead thereof give Credit for £ 19200. 19. 7 1/3 d. Sterling
I am
Gentlemen
To Dr. Nooth & Mallet Esqrs } Your
Purveyors of His Majesty's } most obedient humble servt.
Hospitals } J. G. Lorentz.
translation_english:
Due to the absence of the original documents left behind, I ordered our physicians and apothecaries—who are well aware of which medicines and drugs they brought in 1776 and what they received from home in 1777—to prepare an account of them. I instructed them to include only those items they were prepared to swear an oath upon, charging less rather than more, as any discrepancy would easily be discovered once the original vouchers were available.
Following these orders, I received the following German accounts for medicine and drugs, namely:
In the General Account for the year 1776, under Article X:
a) For Medicines, Drugs, etc. (in German Money):
To Mr. Wild, Apothecary at Cassel, No. 32, for: 3054 Rthl. 9 gr. 8 pf.
To the same, No. 33: 1674 Rthl. 11 gr. 3 pf.
c) For old linen, bandages, etc., for the use of the surgeons:
To Mr. Bonsfeld, Merchant at Cassel, No. 44: 378 Rthl. 20 gr. 3 pf.
To the same, No. 45: 263 Rthl. 12 gr.
In the year 1777, under Article XI:
No. 92, at 4920 Rthl. 31 alb., or: 4920 Rthl. 23 gr.
Totaling in all: 10291 Rthl. 18 gr. 9 pf.
At the same time, I wrote home for the original vouchers for these medicines and drugs for both 1776 and 1777. I received them by the last mail. You will find them attached here, with the handwriting matching the original vouchers of this year's accounts already in your possession, which I received earlier with the last Hessian recruits.
Upon examining the aforementioned originals, I find they differ from my substituted vouchers by 2701 Rthl. 20 1/4 ggr, or £ 463. 3s. 6d. Sterling. The total sum of the originals amounts to 12993 Rthl. 15 ggr, while only 10291 Rthl. 18 gr. 9 pf. was charged.
I hope, Gentlemen, you will be pleased to add the aforementioned £ 463. 3s. 6d. to the total demand of the Hessian Hospital, which is £ 18,737. 16s. 1 1/3d., and instead give credit for £ 19,200. 19s. 7 1/3d. Sterling.
I am, Gentlemen, your most obedient humble servant, J. G. Lorentz.
To Dr. Nooth & Mallet Esqrs, Purveyors of His Majesty's Hospitals.
notes: The document is written in 18th-century English cursive. It details financial accounting for medical supplies for the Hessian troops during the American Revolutionary War. Currency is listed in Reichsthaler (Rthl), Groschen (gr), Pfennig (pf), Albus (alb), and British Pounds Sterling (£/s/d).
